payload.message: is anything missing from the performance plan?Anything we did not consider yet? anything too fuzzy?

Yes. The plan is solid, but a few things are still missing or too fuzzy.

Missing:
- A baseline measurement procedure.
  - Define exactly where to measure: game route idle, game route driving, settings route open.
  - Define how long to sample and what counts as improvement.
- A target budget.
  - Example: `~16.7 ms/frame` for 60 FPS, or a specific recovery target relative to the pre-regression build.
- A distinction between CPU, GPU, and compositor cost.
  - Right now the plan mostly assumes main-thread/UI cost, which is probably right, but not explicitly separated.
- A rollback/ablation strategy.
  - Example: disable blur only, then disable DOM updates only, then pause settings rendering only, so each change can be isolated.
- A “don’t regress correctness” checklist.
  - Route behavior, theme persistence, controls, FPS monitor accuracy, and game feel should be explicitly rechecked after each perf change.

Not yet considered enough:
- Text rendering cost.
  - Repeated text updates can still cause layout/paint even without `innerHTML`.
- The cost of the perf monitor itself.
  - It may materially affect the numbers while measuring performance.
- Browser/device variance.
  - Blur/compositing cost can differ a lot across browsers and GPUs.
- GPU/compositor throttling from transparency layers.
  - Not just `backdrop-filter`, but also stacked translucent panels and shadows.
- Whether rendering should be skipped entirely on unchanged UI states.
  - For example, lower-frequency UI updates or pausing parts of the overlay.
- Whether the canvas resolution or DPR is contributing.
  - High `devicePixelRatio` can quietly make rendering much more expensive.

Too fuzzy:
- “Pause or reduce render frequency if possible”
  - This should be a concrete decision tree.
  - Example: on `#/settings`, either fully pause simulation and render once, or simulate at 5 Hz, or keep live background at 30 FPS.
- “Stop updating HUD/perf content when values have not meaningfully changed”
  - Needs thresholds.
  - Example: only update FPS text 4 times/sec; only update speed when delta exceeds `0.1`.
- “Reassess whether `PHYSICS_SUBSTEPS = 10` is still justified”
  - Needs a test method.
  - Example: compare `10`, `8`, `6`, `4` under the same drive scenario and record stability + FPS.
- “Collision checks can be reduced”
  - The current plan doesn’t say how to measure collision workload or what simplification is acceptable.

What I’d add:
1. A baseline section with 3 test scenarios and a template for recording FPS/frame/input/physics/render/UI.
2. A perf-hypothesis matrix:
   - blur/compositing
   - HUD DOM churn
   - perf panel DOM churn
   - settings route full simulation
   - DPR/canvas resolution
3. A strict ablation order so changes are isolated one at a time.
4. A target outcome section with concrete numbers.
5. A note that the perf monitor itself should have a low-overhead mode or reduced update cadence during measurement.

So: nothing fundamentally wrong, but it still needs sharper measurement methodology and more explicit decisions about how to evaluate each suspected bottleneck.

*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /Users/wizard23/projects/asimov/minimal-npm-workspace/docs/plans/2026-03-19--14-09--performance-improvement-plan.md
@@
 ## Goal
 
 Improve frontend performance without changing the product direction:
@@
 
 The immediate objective is to recover the framerate regression introduced by the heavier UI layer.
+
+Primary target:
+
+- sustained `60 FPS`
 
 ## Current Performance Risks
@@
 4. running full simulation and rendering while on the settings route
 5. extra style/layout/compositing cost from more UI elements
 
 ## Measurement First
 
-Before optimizing, preserve and extend measurement quality.
+Before optimizing, preserve and extend measurement quality. Measurements must be concrete and repeatable.
 
-Add or confirm measurements for:
+### Required Core Measurements
+
+Measure these separately:
 
-- real frame time
 - real FPS
-- input CPU
-- physics CPU
-- render CPU
-- UI update CPU
-- route state
-- whether settings are open
-- number of DOM updates per frame
+- game engine / physics time
+- render time
+
+Additional measurements if useful:
+
+- UI update time
+- route state
+- whether settings/editor UI is visible
+- number of DOM updates per frame
 
 Important:
 
-- distinguish CPU-side render submission from true frame rate
-- avoid confusing labels
+- sample each scenario for at least `10 seconds`
+- define results per scenario, not just single-frame snapshots
+- avoid confusing labels
+
+### Measurement Modes
+
+We need to distinguish between:
+
+1. real game mode
+   - only the canvas remains
+   - every additional DOM element is removed
+   - main loop should have `0` DOM updates
+   - performance monitor may exist only if it updates at a very low cadence
+2. configure / track editor mode
+   - additional DOM is allowed
+   - this mode may carry more UI cost
+
+### Baseline Test Scenarios
+
+At minimum, record measurements for:
+
+1. game mode, idle
+2. game mode, driving
+3. configure/settings mode open
+
+For each scenario, sample at least `10 seconds` and record:
+
+- average FPS
+- average frame time
+- average physics time
+- average render time
+- whether DOM updates occur in the main loop
+
+### Improvement Criteria
+
+An optimization counts as useful if it improves one or more of:
+
+- average FPS
+- average frame time
+- average physics time
+- average render time
+
+without breaking behavior.
 
 ## Optimization Order
@@
 3. Stop updating HUD/perf content when values have not meaningfully changed.
 
 Expected payoff:
@@
 ### Phase 2: Route-Aware Work Reduction
 
 When on `#/settings`:
 
 - pause or heavily reduce physics updates
 - pause or reduce render frequency if possible
 - avoid unnecessary HUD updates
 - consider pausing gameplay entirely unless there is a reason to keep it live
 
 Expected payoff:
 
 - settings route becomes materially cheaper
 - UI navigation no longer carries full game-loop cost
+
+Concrete requirement:
+
+- real game mode should have `0` DOM updates in the main loop
 
 ### Phase 3: UI Architecture Cleanup
@@
 Targets:
 
 - separate HUD rendering from game rendering
 - separate performance monitor rendering from frame loop
-- update monitor on a slower cadence, for example 4-10 times per second
+- update monitor on a slower cadence, defaulting to about once per second and making that configurable
 - avoid rebuilding settings UI after initial render
 
 Expected payoff:
@@
 After UI/compositing fixes, reassess the game loop itself.
 
 Check:
 
 - whether `PHYSICS_SUBSTEPS = 10` is still justified
-- whether collision checks can be reduced
 - whether route state should influence simulation cadence
 - whether some calculations can be skipped at very low speeds
 
 Expected payoff:
 
 - lower CPU cost in physics-heavy scenes
+
+Priority note:
+
+- collision-check reduction is low priority for now
 
 ## Concrete Tasks
 
 ### Task Group A: Improve Instrumentation
 
 - add `uiUpdateMs`
 - count HUD updates
 - count perf monitor updates
 - log whether the current route is `game` or `settings`
 - add a simple switch to compare blurred vs non-blurred panels
+- add a configurable monitor refresh interval
+
+If useful and practical:
+
+- try to distinguish CPU, GPU, and compositor cost
+
+This is optional unless it helps reach `60 FPS`.
 
 ### Task Group B: Reduce UI Churn
 
 - create fixed DOM nodes for HUD values
 - create fixed DOM nodes for performance values
 - update only text content
 - stop rewriting whole panels every frame
+- ensure real game mode has `0` DOM updates in the main loop
 
 ### Task Group C: Reduce Settings Route Cost
 
 - define expected behavior on settings route
 - if live game is not required there, pause simulation
 - if the background scene should remain visible, consider low-frequency rendering
+- distinguish clearly between pure game mode and configure/editor mode
 
 ### Task Group D: Re-test
 
 After each change:
 
 1. compare FPS on game route
 2. compare FPS on settings route
 3. compare CPU timings
 4. check that controls, themes, and routing still work
+
+These need to be real tests, not informal observation.
+
+### Task Group E: Text Rendering Test
+
+Write a focused test to measure the cost of text/UI updates.
+
+Goal:
+
+- determine whether repeated text updates are materially affecting frame rate even after removing `innerHTML` churn
+
+### Task Group F: Perf Monitor Cost Control
+
+- update the performance monitor only about once per second
+- make the update cadence configurable
+- ensure the monitor itself does not materially distort measurements
+
+### Task Group G: Physics Performance Tests
+
+Write one or more tests to measure physics cost directly.
+
+Goals:
+
+- measure the effect of `PHYSICS_SUBSTEPS = 10`
+- compare multiple substep counts
+- provide a basis for later optimization work
+
+This should be treated as a real benchmark task, not guesswork.
+
+### Task Group H: Correctness Regression Tests
+
+Create real tests or repeatable checks for:
+
+- route behavior
+- theme persistence
+- controls
+- FPS monitor accuracy
+- game feel / basic playability
+
+Each performance change should be checked against these so speed improvements do not quietly break the app.
 
 ## Acceptance Criteria
 
 The performance work is successful when:
 
 - game route framerate is back near pre-regression levels
 - settings route is cheaper than game route
+- game mode has `0` DOM updates in the main loop
 - FPS monitor still reports real FPS
 - theme selection still works
 - routing still works
 - build and lint remain clean
*** End Patch

content.text: is anything missing from the performance plan?Anything we did not consider yet? anything too fuzzy?

payload.message: is anything missing from the performance plan?Anything we did not consider yet? anything too fuzzy?

A few things are still missing or could be sharper.

Missing:
- A precise benchmark harness definition.
  - Who drives the car during the 10-second sample?
  - Manual driving is noisy. You probably want a deterministic replay/input script for at least one driving benchmark.
- Exact reporting format.
  - Define whether results are average, median, p95, min/max.
  - For perf work, `average + p95` is usually more useful than average alone.
- A clear “pass/fail” threshold per scenario.
  - Example: `game mode driving >= 60 FPS average and no obvious stutter spikes`.
- A way to count DOM updates in a trustworthy way.
  - “0 DOM updates” is good as a goal, but the plan should define how that is measured.
- A way to compare before/after builds.
  - The plan should say where benchmark results are stored so changes are traceable.

Not considered enough yet:
- GC / allocation pressure.
  - Right now the app creates strings and arrays in the loop. Even if average time looks fine, allocations can cause stutter.
- WebGL buffer/data churn.
  - `bufferData` every draw can become a cost; the plan currently focuses more on DOM/UI than GL submission patterns.
- Hidden-tab / background behavior.
  - If the tab is not visible, should the app pause or throttle? Easy win, not mentioned.
- Perf monitor measurement overhead.
  - You mention slower updates, which is good, but not whether the timing collection itself should be stripped down in “true benchmark mode.”
- Mobile / high-DPI behavior.
  - You marked DPR low priority, which is reasonable, but if the device is Retina/high-DPI it can dominate render cost surprisingly early.

Too fuzzy:
- “Real tests” for game feel.
  - That needs a concrete definition. Otherwise it stays subjective.
  - Better: basic smoke checks plus a short manual acceptance checklist.
- “Try to distinguish CPU, GPU, and compositor cost if useful.”
  - Reasonable, but currently vague. Better to say:
  - only pursue this if Phase 1 changes do not recover enough FPS.
- “Configure / track editor mode.”
  - There is no track editor yet. The plan should probably just say “settings/configuration mode” for now.
- “Pause or heavily reduce physics updates” on settings.
  - Needs an explicit product decision:
  - fully pause simulation, or
  - keep a low-FPS animated background.
- “Measure render time.”
  - You should specify whether that means CPU submission time only, since true GPU timing may not be practical here.

What I would add:
1. Deterministic benchmark scenarios.
   - `idle`, `autodrive lap`, `settings open`
2. Result format.
   - `avg FPS`, `p95 frame ms`, `avg physics ms`, `avg render CPU ms`
3. Benchmark mode.
   - monitor updates minimized, optional no-HUD mode
4. Allocation/GC check.
   - track object/string churn in the loop where practical
5. Visibility policy.
   - throttle or pause when tab is hidden

So: the plan is much better now, but it still needs more rigor around repeatability, result reporting, and a few non-DOM causes like allocation churn and WebGL submission patterns.

content.text:
A few things are still missing or could be sharper.

Missing:
- A precise benchmark harness definition.
  - Who drives the car during the 10-second sample?
  - Manual driving is noisy. You probably want a deterministic replay/input script for at least one driving benchmark.
- Exact reporting format.
  - Define whether results are average, median, p95, min/max.
  - For perf work, `average + p95` is usually more useful than average alone.
- A clear “pass/fail” threshold per scenario.
  - Example: `game mode driving >= 60 FPS average and no obvious stutter spikes`.
- A way to count DOM updates in a trustworthy way.
  - “0 DOM updates” is good as a goal, but the plan should define how that is measured.
- A way to compare before/after builds.
  - The plan should say where benchmark results are stored so changes are traceable.

Not considered enough yet:
- GC / allocation pressure.
  - Right now the app creates strings and arrays in the loop. Even if average time looks fine, allocations can cause stutter.
- WebGL buffer/data churn.
  - `bufferData` every draw can become a cost; the plan currently focuses more on DOM/UI than GL submission patterns.
- Hidden-tab / background behavior.
  - If the tab is not visible, should the app pause or throttle? Easy win, not mentioned.
- Perf monitor measurement overhead.
  - You mention slower updates, which is good, but not whether the timing collection itself should be stripped down in “true benchmark mode.”
- Mobile / high-DPI behavior.
  - You marked DPR low priority, which is reasonable, but if the device is Retina/high-DPI it can dominate render cost surprisingly early.

Too fuzzy:
- “Real tests” for game feel.
  - That needs a concrete definition. Otherwise it stays subjective.
  - Better: basic smoke checks plus a short manual acceptance checklist.
- “Try to distinguish CPU, GPU, and compositor cost if useful.”
  - Reasonable, but currently vague. Better to say:
  - only pursue this if Phase 1 changes do not recover enough FPS.
- “Configure / track editor mode.”
  - There is no track editor yet. The plan should probably just say “settings/configuration mode” for now.
- “Pause or heavily reduce physics updates” on settings.
  - Needs an explicit product decision:
  - fully pause simulation, or
  - keep a low-FPS animated background.
- “Measure render time.”
  - You should specify whether that means CPU submission time only, since true GPU timing may not be practical here.

What I would add:
1. Deterministic benchmark scenarios.
   - `idle`, `autodrive lap`, `settings open`
2. Result format.
   - `avg FPS`, `p95 frame ms`, `avg physics ms`, `avg render CPU ms`
3. Benchmark mode.
   - monitor updates minimized, optional no-HUD mode
4. Allocation/GC check.
   - track object/string churn in the loop where practical
5. Visibility policy.
   - throttle or pause when tab is hidden

So: the plan is much better now, but it still needs more rigor around repeatability, result reporting, and a few non-DOM causes like allocation churn and WebGL submission patterns.

Chunk ID: 1b5fc7
Wall time: 0.1985 seconds

Output:
# Performance Improvement Plan

## Goal

Improve frontend performance without changing the product direction:

- keep the raw WebGL driving demo
- keep the settings route
- keep theme selection
- keep the performance monitor

The immediate objective is to recover the framerate regression introduced by the heavier UI layer.

Primary target:

- sustained `60 FPS`

## Current Performance Risks

Known likely bottlenecks:

1. multiple `backdrop-filter: blur(...)` UI panels over a constantly changing WebGL canvas
2. rebuilding HUD HTML every frame with `innerHTML`
3. rebuilding performance monitor HTML every frame with `innerHTML`
4. running full simulation and rendering while on the settings route
5. extra style/layout/compositing cost from more UI elements

## Measurement First

Before optimizing, preserve and extend measurement quality. Measurements must be concrete and repeatable.

### Required Core Measurements

Measure these separately:

- real FPS
- game engine / physics time
- render time

Additional measurements if useful:

- UI update time
- route state
- whether settings/editor UI is visible
- number of DOM updates per frame

Important:

- sample each scenario for at least `10 seconds`
- define results per scenario, not just single-frame snapshots
- avoid confusing labels

### Measurement Modes

We need to distinguish between:

1. real game mode
   - only the canvas remains
   - every additional DOM element is removed
   - main loop should have `0` DOM updates
   - performance monitor may exist only if it updates at a very low cadence
2. configure / track editor mode
   - additional DOM is allowed
   - this mode may carry more UI cost

### Baseline Test Scenarios

At minimum, record measurements for:

1. game mode, idle
2. game mode, driving
3. configure/settings mode open

For each scenario, sample at least `10 seconds` and record:

- average FPS
- average frame time
- average physics time
- average render time
- whether DOM updates occur in the main loop

### Improvement Criteria

An optimization counts as useful if it improves one or more of:

- average FPS
- average frame time
- average physics time
- average render time

without breaking behavior.

## Optimization Order

### Phase 1: Fastest High-Confidence Wins

1. Remove or reduce `backdrop-filter` on floating panels.
2. Replace per-frame `innerHTML` replacement with stable DOM nodes and text updates.
3. Stop updating HUD/perf content when values have not meaningfully changed.

Expected payoff:

- reduced paint/compositing cost
- reduced DOM churn
- lower main-thread work per frame

### Phase 2: Route-Aware Work Reduction

When on `#/settings`:

- pause or heavily reduce physics updates
- pause or reduce render frequency if possible
- avoid unnecessary HUD updates
- consider pausing gameplay entirely unless there is a reason to keep it live

Expected payoff:

- settings route becomes materially cheaper
- UI navigation no longer carries full game-loop cost

Concrete requirement:

- real game mode should have `0` DOM updates in the main loop

### Phase 3: UI Architecture Cleanup

Refactor the current single-file UI logic so UI updates are cheaper and easier to reason about.

Targets:

- separate HUD rendering from game rendering
- separate performance monitor rendering from frame loop
- update monitor on a slower cadence, defaulting to about once per second and making that configurable
- avoid rebuilding settings UI after initial render

Expected payoff:

- less layout and style recalculation
- easier profiling of the remaining hotspots

### Phase 4: Simulation Efficiency

After UI/compositing fixes, reassess the game loop itself.

Check:

- whether `PHYSICS_SUBSTEPS = 10` is still justified
- whether route state should influence simulation cadence
- whether some calculations can be skipped at very low speeds

Expected payoff:

- lower CPU cost in physics-heavy scenes

Priority note:

- collision-check reduction is low priority for now

## Concrete Tasks

### Task Group A: Improve Instrumentation

- add `uiUpdateMs`
- count HUD updates
- count perf monitor updates
- log whether the current route is `game` or `settings`
- add a simple switch to compare blurred vs non-blurred panels
- add a configurable monitor refresh interval

If useful and practical:

- try to distinguish CPU, GPU, and compositor cost

This is optional unless it helps reach `60 FPS`.

### Task Group B: Reduce UI Churn

- create fixed DOM nodes for HUD values
- create fixed DOM nodes for performance values
- update only text content
- stop rewriting whole panels every frame
- ensure real game mode has `0` DOM updates in the main loop

### Task Group C: Reduce Settings Route Cost

- define expected behavior on settings route
- if live game is not required there, pause simulation
- if the background scene should remain visible, consider low-frequency rendering
- distinguish clearly between pure game mode and configure/editor mode

### Task Group D: Re-test

After each change:

1. compare FPS on game route
2. compare FPS on settings route
3. compare CPU timings
4. check that controls, themes, and routing still work

These need to be real tests, not informal observation.

### Task Group E: Text Rendering Test

Write a focused test to measure the cost of text/UI updates.

Goal:

- determine whether repeated text updates are materially affecting frame rate even after removing `innerHTML` churn

### Task Group F: Perf Monitor Cost Control

- update the performance monitor only about once per second
- make the update cadence configurable
- ensure the monitor itself does not materially distort measurements

### Task Group G: Physics Performance Tests

Write one or more tests to measure physics cost directly.

Goals:

- measure the effect of `PHYSICS_SUBSTEPS = 10`
- compare multiple substep counts
- provide a basis for later optimization work

This should be treated as a real benchmark task, not guesswork.

### Task Group H: Correctness Regression Tests

Create real tests or repeatable checks for:

- route behavior
- theme persistence
- controls
- FPS monitor accuracy
- game feel / basic playability

Each performance change should be checked against these so speed improvements do not quietly break the app.

## Acceptance Criteria

The performance work is successful when:

- game route framerate is back near pre-regression levels
- settings route is cheaper than game route
- game mode has `0` DOM updates in the main loop
- FPS monitor still reports real FPS
- theme selection still works
- routing still works
- build and lint remain clean

## Verification Workflow

For code changes, always run:

1. `npm run build`
2. `npm run lint`

If one fails, fix it before moving on.

## Likely First Implementation Pass

The first pass should probably do only these three things:

1. remove or reduce `backdrop-filter`
2. stop using per-frame `innerHTML` updates for HUD and perf monitor
3. pause or throttle the game loop while on `#/settings`

That is the most likely path to recover a large portion of the lost frame rate quickly.

Yes.

The plan is ready for small incremental implementation steps. The first good slice is:

1. Add benchmark mode scaffolding.
   - game mode with `0` DOM updates target
   - perf monitor update interval configurable
   - simple DOM-write counter

   - measure again

4. Pause simulation on `#/settings`.
   - keep behavior simple and explicit
   - measure settings-route cost again

That sequence is small, testable, and aligned with the highest-confidence wins. If you want, I can start with step 1 now.

content.text: I'm not sure that you understood me. The sliders don't work for me. Specifically: When I drag and drop the "ball" on the slieder the dragging does not work. Please explain to me the root cause of this problem befor you change anything pleas.

payload.message: I'm not sure that you understood me. The sliders don't work for me. Specifically: When I drag and drop the "ball" on the slieder the dragging does not work. Please explain to me the root cause of this problem befor you change anything pleas.

Yes. The current root cause is almost certainly this:

- the slider control is re-rendered during its own `input` event
- while you drag the thumb, the browser emits many `input` events
- our handler updates state and then rebuilds the controls DOM
- that destroys and recreates the `<input type="range">` element mid-drag
- as a result, continuous dragging breaks and it feels like only click-to-set works

Concretely, in the current implementation the `Performance` controls section is still being regenerated inside the slider `input` handler for:
- simulation speed
- perf monitor interval

That means the slider you started dragging no longer exists after the first movement event.

So the issue is not the HTML range element itself. It is the control re-render strategy: we are replacing the slider node while the user is dragging it.
